comics: Comics tell stories with sequential art and text.
A “panel” is a single frame of art.
A “gutter” is the space between panels.
Word balloons show dialogue; captions provide narration or context.
Sound effects in comics often use onomatopoeia (e.g., “SNIKT,” “THWIP”).
A splash page is a full-page panel used for emphasis.
An issue is a single installment of a comic series.
A limited series has a set number of issues.
An ongoing series continues indefinitely until canceled or concluded.
A one-shot is a standalone single issue.
Trade paperbacks collect multiple issues into one book.
An omnibus is a large hardcover that collects many issues or runs.
Variant covers are alternate cover designs for the same interior content.
“Newsstand” editions were sold in general retail; “direct” editions were sold to comic shops.
Bag-and-board is standard protective storage for single issues.
CGC is a major company that grades and encapsulates comics.
The grading scale for comics typically ranges from 0.5 to 10.0.
First appearances are often considered “key” issues.
Second appearances and origin issues can also be “keys.”
“Retcon” means retroactive continuity—changing past details in canon.
“Canon” is the accepted continuity of a universe or series.
“Reboot” restarts a continuity or character line.
“Crossover” stories span multiple series or characters.
“Anthology” issues collect multiple short stories.
“Creator-owned” comics let creators retain rights (e.g., many Image titles).
The Golden Age of Comics began in the late 1930s.
The Silver Age followed in the mid-1950s.
The Bronze Age began in the early 1970s.
The Modern Age began in the mid-1980s.
Superman debuted in Action Comics #1 (1938).
Batman debuted in Detective Comics #27 (1939).
Wonder Woman debuted in All Star Comics #8 (1941).
Captain America debuted in Captain America Comics #1 (1941).
Spider-Man debuted in Amazing Fantasy #15 (1962).
The Fantastic Four debuted in Fantastic Four #1 (1961).
The X-Men debuted in The X-Men #1 (1963).
Iron Man debuted in Tales of Suspense #39 (1963).
Thor debuted in Journey into Mystery #83 (1962).
Daredevil debuted in Daredevil #1 (1964).
The Hulk first appeared in The Incredible Hulk #1 (1962).
The Hulk was originally gray; printing issues led to the green color.
The Joker first appeared in Batman #1 (1940).
Lex Luthor appeared early in Superman comics (Golden Age).
Wolverine’s first full appearance is The Incredible Hulk #181 (1974).
Venom’s first full appearance is The Amazing Spider-Man #300 (1988).
Deadpool debuted in The New Mutants #98 (1991).
Black Panther debuted in Fantastic Four #52 (1966).
Barry Allen’s Flash debut in Showcase #4 (1956) helped start the Silver Age.
Hal Jordan’s Green Lantern debuted in Showcase #22 (1959).
“Elseworlds” labels many DC out-of-continuity tales.
Marvel’s “What If…?” explores alternate realities.
The Comics Code Authority (1950s era) once set content guidelines.
The CCA seal used to appear on many covers; it’s now defunct.
Vertigo was DC’s mature-readers imprint for many years.
Image Comics was founded by artists seeking creator ownership (1990s).
Watchmen is a landmark 12-issue DC series (1986–87).
The Dark Knight Returns (1986) reimagined an older Batman.
Crisis on Infinite Earths (1985–86) reorganized DC continuity.
Marvel’s Secret Wars (1984–85) was a major crossover event.
Marvel’s Civil War (2006–07) explored hero vs. hero conflict over registration.
“Trade dress” is the consistent branding/design on a series’ covers.
Foil, embossed, die-cut, and chromium covers were popular in the 1990s.
“Pull lists” let shops reserve selected titles for customers.
Overstreet Price Guide has long been a standard for valuations.
“Slabbed” comics are graded and sealed by a third party.
“Newsprint” was the traditional paper stock for older comics.
Modern comics often use higher-quality, glossy paper.
“Lettercols” (letter columns) previously printed reader letters and replies.
“Cold opens” or teaser pages hook the reader before the credits.
“Four-color process” (CMYK) is standard printing for comics.
Manga are Japanese comics typically read right-to-left.
Tankōbon are collected manga volumes.
Webcomics are published primarily online.
Graphic novels are long-form comics published as books.
Will Eisner helped popularize the term “graphic novel.”
“Decompressed storytelling” uses more pages per scene and slower pacing.
“Compressed storytelling” packs more plot per issue.
“Splash” and “double-splash” pages emphasize big moments.
“Gotham City” is Batman’s primary setting.
“Metropolis” is Superman’s primary setting.
Clark Kent is Superman’s alter ego and a reporter.
Bruce Wayne is Batman’s alter ego and a billionaire industrialist.
Peter Parker is Spider-Man’s alter ego and a photographer/scientist.
“Multiverse” refers to multiple parallel universes in continuity.
Miles Morales is a younger Spider-Man from an alternate universe.
“Antiheroes” (e.g., Punisher) operate outside strict heroic codes.
Deadpool is known for breaking the fourth wall.
“Run” refers to a continuous stint by a creative team or on a title.
“Creator credits” list writer, artist, colorist, letterer, and editor.
“Inkers” refine and define penciled art.
“Colorists” add color; modern work is often digital.
“Letterers” place dialogue, captions, and sound effects.
“House style” describes a publisher’s common visual approach at a time.
Free Comic Book Day is typically held annually to promote the medium.
Omnibuses and deluxe editions often include bonus art and essays.
“Key” lists highlight important issues for collectors.
“Reprints” allow classic stories to be read affordably.
“Creator-owned” titles can move between publishers or be self-published.
“Anthology magazines” were early homes for many iconic heroes.
Comic universes evolve continuously via relaunches, events, and new creators.
cards: Trading cards are printed collectibles, often standard poker size (about 63×88 mm).
Sleeves help protect cards from wear and moisture.
Penny sleeves are thin inner sleeves; toploaders are rigid plastic holders.
“Double-sleeving” uses an inner perfect-fit and an outer sleeve for extra protection.
9-pocket binder pages are common for collection display.
PSA, BGS, and CGC Cards are major third-party grading companies.
Grading scales typically run 1–10; “Gem Mint 10” is the top grade.
Centering, corners, edges, and surface influence a card’s grade.
A slab is a tamper-evident plastic holder for graded cards.
Population reports show how many cards exist at each grade.
Serial-numbered cards (e.g., 1/99) indicate limited print runs.
“Short print” (SP) means fewer copies than typical base cards.
Parallels are alternate versions (often with different colors/foils) of a base card.
Inserts are special subsets with unique designs inserted into packs.
“Refractor,” “Prizm,” and similar terms indicate reflective foil variants.
Holofoil cards use a shiny, light-reflecting layer.
A booster pack contains a random assortment of cards.
A booster box contains multiple sealed packs.
A set is a coordinated release of cards with shared themes and numbering.
A master set includes every card and variant from a release.
“Box topper” is a bonus card included with sealed boxes.
“Case hit” is a rare card expected about once per sealed case.
Retail and hobby products can have different exclusive inserts.
Blaster boxes and hanger packs are common retail formats.
Checklists list a set’s card numbers and subjects.
Artist proofs exist in some non-sport and TCG products.
Sketch cards are original drawings inserted in some sets.
Memorabilia (“patch”) cards contain material like jersey pieces (sports).
“Game-worn” and “player-worn” indicate how memorabilia was used.
Autographed cards can be on-card (signed directly) or sticker autos.
Redemption cards can be exchanged for a card not yet ready at pack-out.
Factory-sealed products command premiums due to guaranteed untampered contents.
Repacked products are resealed third-party assortments (quality varies).
Buying by the “spot” in case breaks distributes cards by team, player, or slot.
eBay and TCGplayer are popular marketplaces for cards.
“Buylist” pricing is what stores pay to acquire cards.
“Comps” are recent comparable sales used to estimate value.
Condition labels like NM, LP, MP, HP, and Damaged are widely used by sellers.
UV light can fade inks; storage away from sunlight helps preservation.
Acid-free materials help protect paper stock over time.
Magic: The Gathering (MTG) launched in 1993.
MTG uses five colors of mana: white, blue, black, red, and green.
MTG card types include creatures, instants, sorceries, enchantments, artifacts, planeswalkers, and lands.
MTG constructed decks generally require a minimum of 60 cards.
Commander is a popular MTG format with 100-card singleton decks and a “commander.”
MTG has a “color pie” defining each color’s strengths and weaknesses.
Mythic rare rarity in MTG sits above rare (introduced later than the original rarities).
Double-faced MTG cards can transform or have two faces.
Tokens represent permanents created by card effects.
The MTG “stack” governs how spells and abilities resolve (last in, first out).
Summoning sickness prevents newly cast creatures from attacking or tapping (unless they have haste).
The Reserved List is a policy promising certain MTG cards won’t be reprinted in tournament-legal form.
Secret Lair is a direct-to-consumer MTG product line with curated reprints.
Full-art lands are a collectible MTG variant with expanded artwork.
Yu-Gi-Oh! is a trading card game published by Konami.
Yu-Gi-Oh! has Monster, Spell, and Trap cards.
Yu-Gi-Oh! monsters have Levels (or Ranks/Link Ratings) shown by stars/arrows.
Monster Attributes include LIGHT, DARK, EARTH, FIRE, WATER, WIND, and DIVINE.
Types include categories like Dragon, Warrior, and Spellcaster.
Yu-Gi-Oh! Extra Deck can include Fusion, Synchro, Xyz, and Link Monsters.
Pendulum Monsters function as both monsters and spells in specific zones.
Yu-Gi-Oh! main decks typically range from 40 to 60 cards.
Yu-Gi-Oh! Side Decks (often up to 15) allow adjustments between games.
Yu-Gi-Oh! commonly starts duels at 8000 Life Points.
First Edition stamps on Yu-Gi-Oh! indicate an early print run.
Unlimited editions lack the First Edition stamp.
Yu-Gi-Oh! maintains Forbidden/Limited lists to balance gameplay.
Yu-Gi-Oh! has rare foiling styles like Ultimate Rare, Ghost Rare, and Starlight Rare.
Different TCGs use set symbols and collector numbers to identify cards.
Artist credit typically appears on TCG card fronts.
Alternate art versions present new illustrations of existing cards.
“Playmats” provide a clean, protective surface for gameplay.
“Deck boxes” store and protect built decks.
Dice and counters track life, damage, or other in-game values.
“Top cut” refers to elimination rounds after Swiss pairings in tournaments.
Swiss pairings match players with similar records each round.
Judges officiate rules at sanctioned events.
Decklists are required at higher-level tournaments.
Proxies are unofficial stand-ins; tournament legality varies by event/policy.
Altered cards are artist-modified; legality depends on rules and clarity.
Many TCGs restrict mixed sleeve colors to prevent marked cards.
Proper shuffling protects sleeves and randomizes decks.
“Pull rate” refers to the statistical odds of opening certain rarities.
Card stock thickness and finish can vary by set and era.
Reprints help make playable cards more accessible.
Reprint symbols or updated set codes distinguish new printings.
Some sets include “box toppers” or buy-a-box promos at release.
Collector boosters (in some TCGs) emphasize premium foils and variants.
Non-sport sets cover franchises like comics, games, TV, and movies.
Rookie cards (sports) are often a player’s most iconic base card.
Cut-signature cards use authentic autographs from documents or memorabilia.
“Game-used” wording on sports inserts indicates use in actual play (when applicable).
“Player-worn” may mean used in a photo shoot rather than a game.
Marketplace “raw” means ungraded cards.
Cracking a slab allows resubmission to another grader (or regrade).
Population scarcity in high grades can affect liquidity and price.
Protective storage in a dry, cool environment helps prevent warping.
Many collectors track inventory with spreadsheets or collection apps.
Licenses and trademarks govern which brands and characters appear on cards.
Community price indices often reflect recent verified sales, not list prices.
coins: A coin is a piece of metal authorized by a government as money.
The obverse is the “heads” side; the reverse is the “tails” side.
The rim is the raised border that helps protect designs.
The edge can be plain, reeded, lettered, or patterned.
A mint mark indicates where a coin was struck.
The U.S. commonly uses P (Philadelphia), D (Denver), S (San Francisco), and W (West Point) mint marks.
Mintage is the number of coins produced for a date/mint.
A planchet is the blank metal disk before striking.
Dies are the hardened steel tools that strike designs into planchets.
Circulated coins show wear from use.
Uncirculated (mint state) coins show no wear from circulation.
Proof coins are struck with special dies and polished planchets for mirrorlike fields.
Proof coins are often struck at least twice for better detail.
Bullion coins are valued primarily for metal content rather than face value.
A troy ounce equals 31.1035 grams.
Silver bullion is commonly .999 fine today.
Many historical silver coins were .900 fine (90% silver).
Gold bullion coins are often .999 or .9999 fine.
The American Gold Eagle is 22-karat (.9167 fine) by design.
The Canadian Maple Leaf is a well-known .9999 fine bullion series.
The South African Krugerrand is a famous gold bullion coin series.
The UK Britannia is a major gold and silver bullion series.
The Chinese Panda is an annually changing bullion design series.
The Austrian Philharmonic is a popular European bullion series.
The Australian Kangaroo is a well-known bullion series from Perth Mint.
Euro coins have a common side and a national side.
Bimetallic coins (e.g., €1, €2) use two different metals in one coin.
World coins use many denominations (peso, rupee, yen, euro, pound, etc.).
Some countries have eliminated their smallest coin and round cash totals.
Gresham’s Law: “Bad money drives out good” under fixed face values.
Seigniorage is the profit a government makes issuing currency.
Intrinsic value is the metal value; numismatic value is collector value.
Cleaning a coin usually reduces its collector value.
Toning is color change from oxidation; it can be natural or artificial.
“Bag marks” are small contact marks from coins jostling together.
Cartwheel luster is a rotating light effect on uncirculated coins.
Milk spots are white blemishes that can appear on some modern silver coins.
Privy marks are small design symbols added for variety or security.
Micro-engraving and radial lines are modern anti-counterfeiting features.
Reeded edges helped deter clipping (shaving metal) on precious-metal coins.
Coin alignment and medal alignment refer to how obverse/reverse are oriented.
Die rotation errors show misaligned obverse/reverse orientations.
Error types include off-center strikes, double strikes, and broadstrikes.
Clipped planchets are errors where the blank is missing a curved section.
Doubled die varieties show doubled design elements from die hubbing.
Repunched mint marks (RPMs) show a mint mark punched more than once.
Overdates show traces of one date punched over another date.
Die states can be seen as dies wear, crack, or polish over time.
A “mule” is struck with mismatched obverse and reverse dies.
Pattern coins are test or trial designs not made for circulation.
Exonumia includes tokens, medals, and other coin-like items not legal tender.
Medals resemble coins but do not carry a face value.
A type set collects one example of each design, not every date.
A series set seeks every date/mint combination within a design type.
“Key dates” are the toughest dates in a series to find.
“Semi-keys” are also scarce but less so than keys.
Ancient Greek coins often depicted gods, symbols, and city emblems.
Roman coins included denominations like the denarius (silver) and aureus (gold).
Many ancient coins were struck by hand and can be off-center.
Traditional Chinese cash coins were cast with square holes.
Some Islamic coinage uses calligraphic inscriptions and Hijri dates.
Japanese coins may date by emperor’s regnal year.
Hebrew, Hijri, and other calendars appear on various world coins.
British pre-decimal denominations included farthing, shilling, and florin.
The UK adopted decimal coinage in 1971.
The U.S. used silver in circulating dimes, quarters, and halves through 1964.
U.S. half dollars contained 40% silver from 1965–1970.
Clad coins sandwich a core metal between outer layers.
Face value is the legal denomination printed on the coin.
Legal tender status does not equate to bullion value.
Many modern commemorative coins are non-circulating legal tender (NCLT).
World mints sell collector coins at a premium above face value.
Coin capsules are clear hard holders for single coins.
Tubes store multiple identical coins (often bullion).
Albums help organize series by date and mint.
Original bank-wrapped rolls contain coins in mint paper or plastic.
Roll hunting means searching coin rolls for silver or varieties.
A Sovereign is a long-running British gold coin series.
The Morgan dollar is a classic large U.S. silver dollar.
The Peace dollar succeeded the Morgan dollar in the early 20th century.
“Junk silver” refers to common-date 90% silver coins valued mainly for silver.
“Mint set” refers to a government-packaged set of circulating-finish coins.
“Proof set” refers to a government-packaged set of proof coins.
Satin or matte finishes have been used on select modern issues.
“Cameo” and “deep cameo” describe frosted devices with mirrored fields on proofs.
Coin specifications (diameter, weight, composition) are set by the issuing mint.
Some coins include edge lettering with mottos or dates.
Magnetic response depends on composition (e.g., steel can be magnetic; silver/gold are not).
An assay tests the purity of a metal item.
Hallmarks indicate purity on bars/jewelry; coins bear official inscriptions instead.
Countermarks are stamps added to coins after original minting.
Many world bullion coins change designs annually (e.g., some pandas, kangaroos).
Security features on modern coins continue to evolve against counterfeiting.
Environmental storage (low humidity, stable temps) helps preserve coins.
PVC in older soft flips can damage coins over time.
A coin’s eye appeal can influence value even at the same grade.
Third-party grading encapsulates coins with a grade and unique certification number.
Registry sets let collectors compete by assembling graded sets.
Not all legal-tender coins are intended for everyday circulation.
Coins combine art, history, metallurgy, and economics in one collectible.
